## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
41	  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
42	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
43	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
44	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
45	  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")
46	
47	## Naming & the home — Manor, Census (settled this session)
48	
49	The federation ledger's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
50	explored and dropped):
51	
52	- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
53	  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
54	  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
55	  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
56	- **Census = the declared ledger** (the intent registry; "Census" = the citizen-roll-with-rank,
57	  bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Census** — resolving the ledger-home question: it
58	  lives in the Payor project where ledger-write authority already sits. Recording AWS-targeting
59	  intent in the Census is not administering AWS (asymmetric sovereignty intact; Manor stays
60	  GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy / multi-Manor becomes real.
61	
62	Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Census**
63	(ledger), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
64	portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.
65	
66	Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
67	is semantic fit, not legacy cost):
68	
69	- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
70	  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
71	- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
72	  Census). **mantle → TBD.**
73	- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.
74	
75	## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)
76	
77	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
78	is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
79	**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
80	Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
81	verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)
82	
83	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
84	
85	The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
86	asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:
87	
88	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
89	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
90	  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.
91	
92	Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
93	revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
94	auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
95	protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
96	by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
97	taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
98	memo.)
99	
100	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
101	
102	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
103	
104	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
105	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
106	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
107	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
108	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
109	  governor buys nothing.
110	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
111	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
112	  are parameters).
113	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
114	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
115	
116	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
117	
118	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
119	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
120	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
121	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
122	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
123	
124	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
125	
126	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
127	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
128	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
129	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
130	
131	## What done looks like
132	
133	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
134	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
135	collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
136	verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
137	throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).
138	
139	The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
140	calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
141	vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
142	deferred — recorded here as shape.
143	
144	## Cinched decisions
145	
146	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
147	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
148	  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
149	  first-class.
150	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
151	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
152	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
153	- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
154	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
155	- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
156	  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
157	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
158	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
159	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
160	  not in this heat.
161	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
162	  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
163	  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
164	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
165	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
166	
167	## Retired cinches
168	
169	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
170	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
171	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
172	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
173	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
174	
175	## Open — resolve within the heat
176	
177	- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
178	  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
179	  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
180	  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
181	  see The audit).
182	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
183	  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
184	- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
185	  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
186	- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
187	  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).
188	
189	## Sources
190	
191	rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
192	split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
193	shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
194	(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
195	points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
196	RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
197	passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
198	the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
  see The audit).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above.

aiTitle: Setup PlantUML automation for federation diagrams

content.text:
Oriented. Here's where things stand.

## Heat BZ — `rbk-14-mvp-citizen-model`

Racing, **groomed, zero paces enrolled.** The paddock is deeply developed (shape cinched across ~10 sections) but execution hasn't started. Its boundary is explicit and load-bearing: *build the **citizen** keyfile tier, structured so its capability layer is identical to the future **federation** tier's — and **do not touch federation here.*** Federation (Workforce pools, STS, device flow) is named OUT of scope.

That matters for your request, and I'll come back to it.

## The PlantUML seam — your idea is well-supported

The crucible already proves the exact mechanism you'd lean on. The `pluml` nameplate runs a PlantUML server on **workstation port 8001**, and fixture case `rbtdrc_pluml_local_diagram` does `POST /txt/uml` with raw `@startuml…@enduml` on stdin — **no hash encoding, no preprocessing.** Swap `txt`→`svg` and the same POST returns SVG bytes. So the core of your tabtarget is one line per source:

```
curl --data-binary @diagram.puml http://localhost:8001/svg/uml > diagram.svg
```

No `.puml` convention exists in the repo yet (only one drawio-exported SVG), so this would establish where sources live and where SVGs commit. Source material for the federation sequences is real and ready: **RBSHR "Operator federation"** (lines ~304-330) and **memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md** (device flow, STS exchange, two-leg delivery).

## My read on tabtarget-vs-test

They're not either/or — they sit at different layers:

- **Tabtarget = the right tool for *generating* committed SVGs.** A test that mutates tracked repo files conflates verification with authoring (interface contamination) — wrong shape for the generation goal.
- **The fixture case is the right tool for *proving the `/svg/uml` endpoint*** the tabtarget rests on. Today the fixture only asserts `/txt`. A one-line `/svg/uml` assertion case would back your authoring tool with a tested seam.

So: build the tabtarget; optionally add a thin SVG-endpoint case so the tool doesn't rest on an untested seam.

## The one genuine fork — where this lands

There's real tension: you said "groom BZ," but BZ's boundary explicitly excludes federation, and you want *federation*-sequence diagrams. The work also splits into two artifacts with possibly two homes — a **reusable pipeline** (federation-agnostic RBK tooling) and **federation design diagrams** (which could orient BZ, or belong to a docs/federation effort).

content.content:
1	# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-27
4	
5	Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
6	per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
7	organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
8	the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
9	single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
10	roadmap entry.
11	
12	NOTE (2026-06-05): the keyfile-tier shape is under active redesign in heat ₣BZ
13	(`Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` — the "citizen" model). The
14	one-SA-per-role and verb-naming assumptions here may change; that memo will fold its
15	divergences in once the model is frozen. Not yet revised.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## The plan
20	
21	RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:
22	
23	- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
24	  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
25	- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
26	  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.
27	
28	A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
29	exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
30	codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
31	requirement, which is also the natural paywall.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Two models, two tiers
36	
37	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
38	|---|---|---|
39	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
40	| GCP organization | not required | required |
41	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
42	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
43	| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |
44	
45	Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
46	Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
47	model-blind.
48	
49	---
50	
51	## The organization requirement is the tier boundary
52	
53	Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
54	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
55	structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.
56	
57	An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
58	creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
59	tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
60	pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
61	verify a domain.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products
66	
67	GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
68	authenticating?*
69	
70	| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
71	|---|---|---|
72	| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
73	| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
74	| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |
75	
76	RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
77	actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.
78	
79	---
80	
81	## How federation authenticates
82	
83	The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
84	touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
85	**never runtime**.
86	
87	**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
88	workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
89	issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
90	claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
91	receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
92	`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
93	Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
94	already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
95	roster.
96	
97	**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
98	IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
99	(RFC 8628):
100	
101	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
102	   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
103	2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
104	   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
105	   is not involved.
106	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
107	
108	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
109	
110	```
111	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
112	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
113	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
114	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
115	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
116	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
117	```
118	
119	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
120	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
121	
122	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
123	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
124	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
125	long session.
126	
127	---
128	
129	## IdP landscape
130	
131	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
132	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
133	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
134	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
135	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
136	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
137	
138	---
139	
140	## Refresh-token policy
141	
142	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
143	Confine it to exactly one place:
144	
145	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
146	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
147	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
148	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
149	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
150	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
151	  token.
152	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
153	  outside the operator mode enum.
154	
155	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
156	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Where configuration lives
161	
162	Three homes, by scope:
163	
164	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
165	  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
166	  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
167	  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
168	  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
169	  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
170	  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
171	  independently straddle modes.
172	- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
173	  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
174	  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
175	- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
176	  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.
177	
178	There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
179	the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
180	to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
181	secret.
182	
183	---
184	
185	## Operator-admin verbs
186	
187	The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
188	**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
189	operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
190	keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
191	on the operator's `principal://` subject.
192	
193	Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
194	**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
195	trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
196	enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.
197	
198	---
199	
200	## One consumer path, real and synthetic
201	
202	Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
203	resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
204	only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
205	device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
206	token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
207	path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.
208	
209	---
210	
211	## The single code seam
212	
213	Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
214	token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —
215	
216	- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
217	- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.
218	
219	Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
220	adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.
221	
222	Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
223	pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
224	enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
225	call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
226	second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
227	again.
228	
229	**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
230	the federation branch.**
231	
232	---
233	
234	## Open at implementation time
235	
236	- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
237	- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
238	  → what the token resolver consumes).
239	- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
240	  calls.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## Sources
245	
246	- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
247	- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
248	- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
249	- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
250	- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
251	- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)
252

exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
requirement, which is also the natural paywall.

---

## Two models, two tiers

| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
|---|---|---|
| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
| GCP organization | not required | required |
| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |

Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
model-blind.

---

## The organization requirement is the tier boundary

Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.

An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
verify a domain.

---

## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products

GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
authenticating?*

| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
|---|---|---|
| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |

RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.

---

## How federation authenticates

The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
**never runtime**.

**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
roster.

**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
(RFC 8628):

1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
   is not involved.
3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.

**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**

```
POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
```

Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.

**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
long session.

---

## IdP landscape

Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.

---

## Refresh-token policy

A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
Confine it to exactly one place:

- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
  token.
- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
  outside the operator mode enum.

A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.

---

## Where configuration lives

Three homes, by scope:

- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
  independently straddle modes.
- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.

There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
secret.

---

## Operator-admin verbs

The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
on the operator's `principal://` subject.

Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.

---

## One consumer path, real and synthetic

Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.

---

## The single code seam

Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —

- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.

Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.

Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
again.

**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
the federation branch.**

---

## Open at implementation time

- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
  → what the token resolver consumes).
- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
  calls.

---

## Sources

- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)

content.text:
Located it precisely.

## The fixture: `canonical-invest` (the "skirmish" variant)

**Source:** `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs` (`RBTDRK_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST`, lines 884-910). It's the **no-levy recycle** variant — it runs against a depot you've already levied (it does *not* create a GCP project), and its whole job is to re-mint the operator credentials. Case sequence (lines 896-902):

1. **`governor_mantle`** — re-mantles the governor against the standing canonical depot, then copies the fresh governor RBRA into `<RBRR_SECRETS_DIR>/governor/rbra.env`.
2. **`director_divest`** → **`retriever_divest`** — revoke-before-delete the standing director/retriever SAs (so the reinvest hits a clean create branch).
3. **`retriever_invest`** → **`director_invest`** — invest fresh SAs, **write the new RBRA key files** to `<RBRR_SECRETS_DIR>/{retriever,director}/rbra.env`, and **access-probe each** (the probe is the proof the new credential reaches GCP).

So a green run does exactly what you described — re-mint governor, recreate director/retriever, and update the RBRA files on disk — and self-verifies the retriever credential, which is the one that just failed the pluml charge.

**How to run it (single fixture):**
```
tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh canonical-invest
```
(It's also bundled in the `skirmish`/`dogfight`/`blockade` suites, but the single fixture is the surgical fix.)

## Two preconditions before it'll pass

- **Standing canonical depot.** `governor_mantle` probes that `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` begins with the canonical family stem (`canest3<tincture>`). Your local GAR images under `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` say this is already installed — but the probe confirms at run time. (If it *weren't* installed, you'd need the gauntlet's `canonical-establish`, which levies a fresh depot.)
- **Valid Payor OAuth (RBRO).** The mantle is Payor→Governor, so it needs a live Payor token. If the Payor OAuth is *also* stale, case 1 fails and you'd run `tt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh` first.

This is an outward-facing GCP operation (deletes and recreates real service accounts, rotates keys), so I won't run it without your go-ahead.

input.content:
@startuml
title Federation Tier — Operator Login (two-leg, curl-native)\nSource: memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md

actor "Operator\n(human)" as OP
participant "RBK Tool\n(workstation)" as TOOL
participant "External IdP\n(OIDC issuer)" as IDP
participant "Browser\n(any device)" as BR
participant "Google STS\nsts.googleapis.com" as STS
participant "Depot API\n(GCP)" as GCP

== Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google, RFC 8628 device flow) ==
OP -> TOOL : invoke (e.g. summon as director)
TOOL -> IDP : POST device-authorization endpoint
IDP --> TOOL : user_code, verification_uri,\ndevice_code, interval
TOOL -> OP : print "go to <uri>, enter WXYZ-1234"
OP -> BR : open verification_uri
BR -> IDP : authorize (prove self to *own* IdP)
note over IDP, BR : Google is **not** involved in Leg 1
loop poll until authorized
  TOOL -> IDP : poll token endpoint (device_code)
end
IDP --> TOOL : OIDC ID token (JWT)

== Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST, RFC 8693) ==
TOOL -> STS : POST /v1/token\ngrant_type=token-exchange\naudience=workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER\nsubject_token=<ID token>  (NO Authorization header)
STS --> TOOL : short-lived Google access_token

== Downstream — model-blind ==
TOOL -> GCP : Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
GCP --> TOOL : depot response
note over TOOL, GCP : Session cap 15 min – 12 h (default 1 h);\nlong runs re-run Leg 2, never one long session

@enduml

note over SETUP, IAM : gcloud / REST touches **setup only**\n(pool & provider creation) — **never runtime**.

@startuml
title The Single Code Seam — one accessor, mode enum branches inside\nSource: memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (heat BZ structural payoff)

participant "Consumer\n(any GCP call site)" as CONS
participant "Token Accessor\n(the one seam fn)" as ACC

CONS -> ACC : token(identity = "director")

alt mode enum == keyfile  (read from RBRD)
  ACC -> RBRA : resolve identity -> SA key
  ACC -> GOOG : signed-JWT exchange
  GOOG --> ACC : access_token
else mode enum == federation
  ACC -> IDP : Leg 1 — device flow (RFC 8628)
  IDP --> ACC : OIDC ID token
  ACC -> STS : Leg 2 — STS token-exchange (RFC 8693)
  STS --> ACC : access_token
end

ACC --> CONS : **bare access_token string**

CONS -> GCP : Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
GCP --> CONS : depot response

note over CONS, GCP : Downstream is **model-blind** — every call site adds Bearer,\nknows nothing of the token's origin.
note over CONS, ACC : Enabling refactor (shippable now, no behavior change):\ncollapse ~30 file-path-keyed call sites behind one\nidentity-keyed accessor. Then federation is just a second branch.

@enduml

content.text: README has a Roadmap appendix with an `#OperatorFederation` tier — that's the natural home for these diagrams. Let me read that section.

content.content: 534 ## Appendix: Roadmap 535 536 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 537 538 - **[Crucible Conduit for Cloud Services](#CrucibleConduit)** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 539 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 540 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 541 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 542 543 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 544 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 545 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 546 547 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - The path to corporate-acceptable identity — admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of long-lived service-account keys on disk, with identity proved by a fresh sign-in and capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret at rest. 548 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 549 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest). 550 551 - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 552 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 553 If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 554 555 - **[Cosign Container Signing](#CosignSigning)** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 556 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 557 558 - **[CDN-Aware IP Gating](#CdnAwareIpGating)** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 559 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 560 561 - **[Podman Support](#PodmanSupport)** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 562 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 563 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 564 565 - **[Crucible-to-Crucible Networking](#CrucibleToCrucible)** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 566 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 567

## What this experiment established

The full loop works and is now permanent, tested infrastructure:

> stale retriever RBRA → `canonical-invest` re-mints all three credentials → pluml charge auto-summons vessels → `POST /svg/uml` renders committed SVGs

- **Pipeline proven** — a live PlantUML crucible renders committed SVGs from `.puml` source with one curl, no encoding. Folded into the pluml fixture as a generative case, so the four federation diagrams stay in lockstep with their source on every fixture run.
- **Four federation critical-sequence diagrams** committed under `diagrams/` (login, setup, keyfile-contrast, the single code seam), drawn faithfully from memo-20260527, and linked from the README Operator Federation roadmap entry.
- **Credential recovery** — `canonical-invest` (the skirmish fixture) is the reusable fix whenever a re-mantled governor strands the director/retriever RBRA files.

No pace was opened (this was heat-affiliated work against ₣BZ, by your instruction), so there's nothing to wrap. The crucible is down and the tree is clean — nothing left running.

One small note for the record: the `diagrams/*.svg` carry a `<?plantuml 1.2026.2?>` version stamp, so a future PlantUML server upgrade in the bottle vessel would make the render case rewrite them (a real, intended diff — the diagrams tracking the renderer), not a flake.
